Airline Route Profitability

2026-04-16

The dataset used in this project is the “Airline Route Profitability Dataset” from Kaggle, available at: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/waleedfaheem/airline-route-profitability-and-cost-analysis?resource=download Author: Muhammad Waleed

library(tidyverse)
## -- Attaching core tidyverse packages ------------------------ tidyverse 2.0.0 --
## v dplyr     1.1.4     v readr     2.1.5
## v forcats   1.0.0     v stringr   1.5.1
## v ggplot2   3.5.2     v tibble    3.3.0
## v lubridate 1.9.4     v tidyr     1.3.1
## v purrr     1.1.0     
## -- Conflicts ------------------------------------------ tidyverse_conflicts() --
## x dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## x dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## i Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library(tidyr)
library(lubridate)
library(patchwork)
#Loading Data
airline_data <- read_csv("airline_route_profitability.csv")
## Rows: 7974 Columns: 33
## -- Column specification --------------------------------------------------------
## Delimiter: ","
## chr   (8): Flight_Number, Origin, Destination, Route, Aircraft_Type, Season,...
## dbl  (24): Aircraft_Capacity, Passengers, Load_Factor, Flight_Hours, Ticket_...
## date  (1): Flight_Date
## 
## i Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## i Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
glimpse(airline_data)
## Rows: 7,974
## Columns: 33
## $ Flight_Number           <chr> "EK8960", "EK3960", "EK7529", "EK4543", "EK311~
## $ Flight_Date             <date> 2024-12-20, 2024-05-13, 2024-10-12, 2024-06-2~
## $ Origin                  <chr> "DXB", "DXB", "DXB", "DXB", "DXB", "DXB", "DXB~
## $ Destination             <chr> "ORD", "HYD", "CDG", "DEL", "RUH", "KWI", "IST~
## $ Route                   <chr> "DXB-ORD", "DXB-HYD", "DXB-CDG", "DXB-DEL", "D~
## $ Aircraft_Type           <chr> "Boeing 777-300ER", "Boeing 787-9", "Boeing 78~
## $ Aircraft_Capacity       <dbl> 396, 296, 296, 296, 180, 180, 296, 189, 180, 2~
## $ Passengers              <dbl> 308, 234, 251, 229, 142, 171, 281, 148, 171, 2~
## $ Load_Factor             <dbl> 0.7791, 0.7910, 0.8502, 0.7748, 0.7901, 0.9500~
## $ Flight_Hours            <dbl> 14.5, 4.2, 7.5, 3.5, 2.2, 2.0, 5.0, 1.5, 2.0, ~
## $ Season                  <chr> "Peak", "Normal", "Shoulder", "Low", "Shoulder~
## $ Route_Category          <chr> "Long Haul", "Medium Haul", "Long Haul", "Medi~
## $ Demand_Level            <chr> "Medium", "Medium", "High", "High", "Medium", ~
## $ Ticket_Revenue          <dbl> 410785.49, 145890.17, 602841.03, 126485.16, 32~
## $ Ancillary_Revenue       <dbl> 53431.94, 17695.27, 78724.16, 17543.26, 3871.6~
## $ Total_Revenue           <dbl> 464217.43, 163585.44, 681565.19, 144028.42, 36~
## $ Fuel_Cost               <dbl> 131033.88, 21338.76, 41011.77, 17333.86, 5576.~
## $ Maintenance_Cost        <dbl> 50750, 9240, 16500, 7700, 2530, 2300, 11000, 1~
## $ Crew_Cost               <dbl> 21750, 5040, 9000, 4200, 1716, 1560, 6000, 120~
## $ Depreciation_Cost       <dbl> 65250, 13440, 24000, 11200, 3740, 3400, 16000,~
## $ Insurance_Cost          <dbl> 11600, 2520, 4500, 2100, 616, 560, 3000, 450, ~
## $ Airport_Fees            <dbl> 4262.30, 2016.63, 10764.79, 6508.04, 4718.58, ~
## $ Catering_Cost           <dbl> 5185.86, 5851.01, 4134.08, 6508.73, 3360.52, 3~
## $ Handling_Cost           <dbl> 4460.11, 5425.84, 3761.85, 4440.93, 3414.18, 5~
## $ Navigation_Fees         <dbl> 10636.90, 3027.25, 3431.70, 2079.79, 1662.49, ~
## $ Sales_Distribution_Cost <dbl> 78193.60, 21586.21, 100188.97, 22686.82, 5133.~
## $ Passenger_Service_Cost  <dbl> 5100.09, 6022.20, 6514.22, 4791.21, 3094.65, 4~
## $ Overhead_Cost           <dbl> 88545.73, 17057.61, 31347.38, 15328.62, 8252.2~
## $ Marketing_Cost          <dbl> 25883.92, 8820.56, 23695.34, 6795.49, 1492.53,~
## $ IT_Systems_Cost         <dbl> 3769.37, 1937.81, 3415.99, 2245.61, 1273.27, 2~
## $ Total_Cost              <dbl> 506421.77, 123323.88, 282266.09, 113919.11, 46~
## $ Profit                  <dbl> -42204.34, 40261.57, 399299.10, 30109.31, -100~
## $ Profit_Margin           <dbl> -9.09, 24.61, 58.59, 20.91, -27.54, 15.75, 27.~
head(airline_data)
## # A tibble: 6 x 33
##   Flight_Number Flight_Date Origin Destination Route   Aircraft_Type   
##   <chr>         <date>      <chr>  <chr>       <chr>   <chr>           
## 1 EK8960        2024-12-20  DXB    ORD         DXB-ORD Boeing 777-300ER
## 2 EK3960        2024-05-13  DXB    HYD         DXB-HYD Boeing 787-9    
## 3 EK7529        2024-10-12  DXB    CDG         DXB-CDG Boeing 787-9    
## 4 EK4543        2024-06-25  DXB    DEL         DXB-DEL Boeing 787-9    
## 5 EK3114        2024-04-20  DXB    RUH         DXB-RUH Airbus A320     
## 6 EK1737        2024-02-06  DXB    KWI         DXB-KWI Airbus A320     
## # i 27 more variables: Aircraft_Capacity <dbl>, Passengers <dbl>,
## #   Load_Factor <dbl>, Flight_Hours <dbl>, Season <chr>, Route_Category <chr>,
## #   Demand_Level <chr>, Ticket_Revenue <dbl>, Ancillary_Revenue <dbl>,
## #   Total_Revenue <dbl>, Fuel_Cost <dbl>, Maintenance_Cost <dbl>,
## #   Crew_Cost <dbl>, Depreciation_Cost <dbl>, Insurance_Cost <dbl>,
## #   Airport_Fees <dbl>, Catering_Cost <dbl>, Handling_Cost <dbl>,
## #   Navigation_Fees <dbl>, Sales_Distribution_Cost <dbl>, ...
# Check missing values before
colSums(is.na(airline_data))
##           Flight_Number             Flight_Date                  Origin 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##             Destination                   Route           Aircraft_Type 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##       Aircraft_Capacity              Passengers             Load_Factor 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##            Flight_Hours                  Season          Route_Category 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##            Demand_Level          Ticket_Revenue       Ancillary_Revenue 
##                       0                       0                     271 
##           Total_Revenue               Fuel_Cost        Maintenance_Cost 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##               Crew_Cost       Depreciation_Cost          Insurance_Cost 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##            Airport_Fees           Catering_Cost           Handling_Cost 
##                       0                     265                     256 
##         Navigation_Fees Sales_Distribution_Cost  Passenger_Service_Cost 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##           Overhead_Cost          Marketing_Cost         IT_Systems_Cost 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##              Total_Cost                  Profit           Profit_Margin 
##                       0                       0                       0
# Replace NAs in numeric columns with the median
airline_clean <- airline_data %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric),
                ~ ifelse(is.na(.), median(., na.rm = TRUE), .)))

# Replace NAs in text columns with "Unknown"
airline_clean <- airline_clean %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.character),
                ~ ifelse(is.na(.), "Unknown", .)))

# Check missing values after
colSums(is.na(airline_clean))
##           Flight_Number             Flight_Date                  Origin 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##             Destination                   Route           Aircraft_Type 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##       Aircraft_Capacity              Passengers             Load_Factor 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##            Flight_Hours                  Season          Route_Category 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##            Demand_Level          Ticket_Revenue       Ancillary_Revenue 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##           Total_Revenue               Fuel_Cost        Maintenance_Cost 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##               Crew_Cost       Depreciation_Cost          Insurance_Cost 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##            Airport_Fees           Catering_Cost           Handling_Cost 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##         Navigation_Fees Sales_Distribution_Cost  Passenger_Service_Cost 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##           Overhead_Cost          Marketing_Cost         IT_Systems_Cost 
##                       0                       0                       0 
##              Total_Cost                  Profit           Profit_Margin 
##                       0                       0                       0
glimpse(airline_clean)
## Rows: 7,974
## Columns: 33
## $ Flight_Number           <chr> "EK8960", "EK3960", "EK7529", "EK4543", "EK311~
## $ Flight_Date             <date> 2024-12-20, 2024-05-13, 2024-10-12, 2024-06-2~
## $ Origin                  <chr> "DXB", "DXB", "DXB", "DXB", "DXB", "DXB", "DXB~
## $ Destination             <chr> "ORD", "HYD", "CDG", "DEL", "RUH", "KWI", "IST~
## $ Route                   <chr> "DXB-ORD", "DXB-HYD", "DXB-CDG", "DXB-DEL", "D~
## $ Aircraft_Type           <chr> "Boeing 777-300ER", "Boeing 787-9", "Boeing 78~
## $ Aircraft_Capacity       <dbl> 396, 296, 296, 296, 180, 180, 296, 189, 180, 2~
## $ Passengers              <dbl> 308, 234, 251, 229, 142, 171, 281, 148, 171, 2~
## $ Load_Factor             <dbl> 0.7791, 0.7910, 0.8502, 0.7748, 0.7901, 0.9500~
## $ Flight_Hours            <dbl> 14.5, 4.2, 7.5, 3.5, 2.2, 2.0, 5.0, 1.5, 2.0, ~
## $ Season                  <chr> "Peak", "Normal", "Shoulder", "Low", "Shoulder~
## $ Route_Category          <chr> "Long Haul", "Medium Haul", "Long Haul", "Medi~
## $ Demand_Level            <chr> "Medium", "Medium", "High", "High", "Medium", ~
## $ Ticket_Revenue          <dbl> 410785.49, 145890.17, 602841.03, 126485.16, 32~
## $ Ancillary_Revenue       <dbl> 53431.94, 17695.27, 78724.16, 17543.26, 3871.6~
## $ Total_Revenue           <dbl> 464217.43, 163585.44, 681565.19, 144028.42, 36~
## $ Fuel_Cost               <dbl> 131033.88, 21338.76, 41011.77, 17333.86, 5576.~
## $ Maintenance_Cost        <dbl> 50750, 9240, 16500, 7700, 2530, 2300, 11000, 1~
## $ Crew_Cost               <dbl> 21750, 5040, 9000, 4200, 1716, 1560, 6000, 120~
## $ Depreciation_Cost       <dbl> 65250, 13440, 24000, 11200, 3740, 3400, 16000,~
## $ Insurance_Cost          <dbl> 11600, 2520, 4500, 2100, 616, 560, 3000, 450, ~
## $ Airport_Fees            <dbl> 4262.30, 2016.63, 10764.79, 6508.04, 4718.58, ~
## $ Catering_Cost           <dbl> 5185.86, 5851.01, 4134.08, 6508.73, 3360.52, 3~
## $ Handling_Cost           <dbl> 4460.11, 5425.84, 3761.85, 4440.93, 3414.18, 5~
## $ Navigation_Fees         <dbl> 10636.90, 3027.25, 3431.70, 2079.79, 1662.49, ~
## $ Sales_Distribution_Cost <dbl> 78193.60, 21586.21, 100188.97, 22686.82, 5133.~
## $ Passenger_Service_Cost  <dbl> 5100.09, 6022.20, 6514.22, 4791.21, 3094.65, 4~
## $ Overhead_Cost           <dbl> 88545.73, 17057.61, 31347.38, 15328.62, 8252.2~
## $ Marketing_Cost          <dbl> 25883.92, 8820.56, 23695.34, 6795.49, 1492.53,~
## $ IT_Systems_Cost         <dbl> 3769.37, 1937.81, 3415.99, 2245.61, 1273.27, 2~
## $ Total_Cost              <dbl> 506421.77, 123323.88, 282266.09, 113919.11, 46~
## $ Profit                  <dbl> -42204.34, 40261.57, 399299.10, 30109.31, -100~
## $ Profit_Margin           <dbl> -9.09, 24.61, 58.59, 20.91, -27.54, 15.75, 27.~

Key Variables:

  • Profit: Total revenue minus total costs for a given flight (in USD).
  • Profit_Margin: Profit as a percentage of Total Revenue (Profit / Total_Revenue x 100). This is a fairer efficiency metric than raw Profit, since long-haul flights earn more absolute profit but may be less efficient per dollar of revenue.
  • Load_Factor: Proportion of seats filled (Passengers / Aircraft Capacity). A value of 1.0 means the flight was completely full.
  • Route_Category: Flight distance tier. Short Haul (1 to 3.5 hrs), Medium Haul (2.5 to 7 hrs), Long Haul (6.5 to 16.5 hrs).
  • Season: Travel demand period. Peak (June to Aug, late Dec), Shoulder (Apr to May, Sep to Oct), Normal (Mar, Nov), Low (Jan to Feb).
  • Demand_Level: Demand classification for a given flight (High, Medium, Low).

Note: The dataset already includes a Profit_Margin column (Profit / Total_Revenue x 100). This is a fairer efficiency metric than raw Profit, since long-haul flights earn more absolute profit but may be less efficient per dollar of revenue.

Exploratory Analysis

Before getting into the research questions, here’s a quick look at average profit across the main grouping variables.

#Average profit by season
airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Season) %>%
  summarize(avg_profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE))
## # A tibble: 4 x 2
##   Season   avg_profit
##   <chr>         <dbl>
## 1 Low          25903.
## 2 Normal       63480.
## 3 Peak        110279.
## 4 Shoulder     82675.

Profit is highest in Peak season and lowest in Low season, which makes sense since more demand generally means fuller flights and higher ticket prices.

# Average profit and profit margin by route category
airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Route_Category) %>%
  summarize(
    avg_profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_margin = mean(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
## # A tibble: 3 x 3
##   Route_Category avg_profit avg_margin
##   <chr>               <dbl>      <dbl>
## 1 Long Haul         160410.       22.7
## 2 Medium Haul        30232.       13.0
## 3 Short Haul         -8841.      -26.1
#Short Haul = 1 to 3.5 hours
#Medium Haul = 2.5 to 7 hours
#Long Haul = 6.5 to 16.5 hours

Long-haul routes earn the most absolute profit, but the margin column tells a more nuanced story, which we explore further in the research questions.

#Average profit and profit margin by demand level
airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Demand_Level) %>%
  summarize(
    avg_profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_margin = mean(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
## # A tibble: 2 x 3
##   Demand_Level avg_profit avg_margin
##   <chr>             <dbl>      <dbl>
## 1 High            107464.      17.7 
## 2 Medium           23302.      -9.73

High demand flights are consistently more profitable in both metrics. This is one of the clearest patterns in the dataset.

# Convert Flight_Date to a proper date
airline_clean <- airline_clean %>%
  mutate(Flight_Date = ymd(Flight_Date))

# Calculate normalized counts
season_counts <- airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Season) %>%
  summarize(
    total_flights = n(),
    days_in_season = n_distinct(Flight_Date)
  ) %>%
  mutate(flights_per_day = total_flights / days_in_season)

# Chart 1: Raw counts (misleading)
plot_raw <- ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Season)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "tomato") +
  labs(
    title = "Before: Raw Flight Counts",
    subtitle = "Looks like Peak has way more flights",
    x = "Season",
    y = "Number of Flights"
  )

# Chart 2: Normalized (fair comparison)
plot_normalized <- ggplot(season_counts, aes(x = Season, y = flights_per_day)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "After: Flights per Day",
    subtitle = "Actually roughly equal across seasons",
    x = "Season",
    y = "Flights per Day"
  )

# Put them side by side
plot_raw + plot_normalized

The raw count makes Peak look dominant, but once normalized by days in each season, flights are actually spread pretty evenly. This is an important data integrity check before drawing conclusions from season data.

Peak (June to August, late December): Busiest travel period with the highest demand. Covers summer vacation and winter holidays. Flights are more expensive and airports are crowded.

Shoulder (April to May, September to October): Transition period between peak and low seasons. Demand and prices are moderate, with fewer crowds than peak times.

Normal (March, November): Average travel demand. Not busy or quiet, with standard pricing.

Low (January to February): Slowest travel period with the lowest demand. Flights are usually cheaper and less crowded.

Research Questions

1. How does profit spread across all flights?

ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Profit)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 50000, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::dollar_format(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "K")) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Profit Across Flights",
    x = "Profit (USD)",
    y = "Number of Flights"
  )

Most flights earn between $0 and $500K. The distribution is right-skewed, meaning a small number of high-performing flights earn significantly more than the rest, with the bulk of the data reaching close to $700K. There are also flights with negative profit, meaning they are operating at a loss.

2. Are long-haul routes more profitable than short-haul routes?

airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Route_Category) %>%
  summarize(
    avg_profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_margin = mean(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
## # A tibble: 3 x 3
##   Route_Category avg_profit avg_margin
##   <chr>               <dbl>      <dbl>
## 1 Long Haul         160410.       22.7
## 2 Medium Haul        30232.       13.0
## 3 Short Haul         -8841.      -26.1
# Profit vs Profit Margin by Route Category (side by side)
plot_profit_route <- ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Route_Category, y = Profit)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "tomato") +
  labs(title = "Profit by Route Category", x = "Route Category", y = "Profit (USD)")

plot_margin_route <- ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Route_Category, y = Profit_Margin)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "Profit Margin by Route Category", x = "Route Category", y = "Profit Margin (%)")

plot_profit_route + plot_margin_route

Long-haul routes earn more absolute profit, but their margin is not dramatically higher. Short-haul routes are smaller in dollar terms but can be just as efficient. So yes, long-haul is more profitable in total dollars, but not because it is more efficient. Mostly it is because the revenue is larger to begin with.

3. Do flights generate more profit during Peak season?

ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Season, y = Profit)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue", color = "navy") +
  labs(
    title = "Profit by Season",
    x = "Season",
    y = "Profit (USD)"
  )

Peak season does show higher median profit, but the boxes overlap quite a bit. This suggests that season alone does not determine whether a flight is profitable, since other factors like demand level and load factor also play a role.

4. How does demand level influence profitability?

airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Demand_Level) %>%
  summarize(
    flight_count = n(),
    avg_profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_profit = median(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_margin = mean(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_margin = median(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
## # A tibble: 2 x 6
##   Demand_Level flight_count avg_profit median_profit avg_margin median_margin
##   <chr>               <int>      <dbl>         <dbl>      <dbl>         <dbl>
## 1 High                 4630    107464.        43018.      17.7          21.9 
## 2 Medium               3344     23302.         2530.      -9.73          1.67
plot_profit_demand <- ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Demand_Level, y = Profit)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "tomato") +
  labs(title = "Profit by Demand Level", x = "Demand Level", y = "Profit (USD)")

plot_margin_demand <- ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Demand_Level, y = Profit_Margin)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "Profit Margin by Demand Level", x = "Demand Level", y = "Profit Margin (%)")

plot_profit_demand + plot_margin_demand

This is one of the strongest findings. High demand flights are clearly more profitable in both absolute and margin terms. The separation between groups is much cleaner here than in the season analysis.

5. Do fuller flights lead to higher profit?

#Load Factor VS Profit
ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Load_Factor, y = Profit)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 1/3, color = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Load Factor vs Profit",
    x = "Load Factor (Passengers / Aircraft Capacity)",
    y = "Profit (USD)"
  )

ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Load_Factor, y = Profit)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Load Factor vs Profit (with Break-Even Line)",
    subtitle = "Red dashed line = break-even (Profit = $0)",
    x = "Load Factor (Passengers / Aircraft Capacity)",
    y = "Profit (USD)"
  )

All flights in this dataset already have a high load factor, so the data looks compact and clustered. Even then, a lot of flights still fall below the break-even line. This tells us that load factor alone does not guarantee profitability. Other fixed costs like fuel, crew salaries, and airport fees also play a significant role in determining whether a flight makes money.

6. Is profit more influenced by season or by demand level?

# by season
airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Season) %>%
  summarize(
    avg_profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_margin = mean(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
## # A tibble: 4 x 3
##   Season   avg_profit avg_margin
##   <chr>         <dbl>      <dbl>
## 1 Low          25903.      -8.50
## 2 Normal       63480.       3.33
## 3 Peak        110279.      17.5 
## 4 Shoulder     82675.      10.1
# by demand
airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Demand_Level) %>%
  summarize(
    avg_profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_margin = mean(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
## # A tibble: 2 x 3
##   Demand_Level avg_profit avg_margin
##   <chr>             <dbl>      <dbl>
## 1 High            107464.      17.7 
## 2 Medium           23302.      -9.73
# Profit vs Profit Margin by Season (side by side)
plot_profit_season <- ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Season, y = Profit)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "tomato") +
  labs(title = "Profit by Season", x = "Season", y = "Profit (USD)")

plot_margin_season <- ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Season, y = Profit_Margin)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "Profit Margin by Season", x = "Season", y = "Profit Margin (%)")

plot_profit_season + plot_margin_season

Demand level shows a cleaner separation between groups than season does. Season has an effect, but a high demand flight in Low season can still outperform a low demand flight in Peak season. Demand level is the stronger driver.

7. Which season and route combinations are most profitable?

airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Season, Route_Category) %>%
  summarize(
    avg_profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_margin = mean(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
## # A tibble: 12 x 4
##    Season   Route_Category avg_profit avg_margin
##    <chr>    <chr>               <dbl>      <dbl>
##  1 Low      Long Haul          70507.      9.95 
##  2 Low      Medium Haul         4013.     -0.320
##  3 Low      Short Haul        -14788.    -45.9  
##  4 Normal   Long Haul         149345.     22.1  
##  5 Normal   Medium Haul        20027.      9.44 
##  6 Normal   Short Haul        -10817.    -30.8  
##  7 Peak     Long Haul         230395.     31.5  
##  8 Peak     Medium Haul        55383.     23.7  
##  9 Peak     Short Haul         -3500.    -10.5  
## 10 Shoulder Long Haul         181076.     26.0  
## 11 Shoulder Medium Haul        36261.     16.7  
## 12 Shoulder Short Haul         -7430.    -20.7

Peak season combined with Long Haul consistently produces the highest average profit and margin. Low season combined with Short Haul is the weakest combination. Airlines should prioritize long-haul scheduling during peak periods.

8. How does profit change month by month?

airline_clean %>%
  mutate(Month = floor_date(Flight_Date, "month")) %>%
  group_by(Month) %>%
  summarize(avg_profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Month, y = avg_profit)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "navy", size = 2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Average Monthly Profit Over Time",
    subtitle = "Red dashed line = break-even",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Average Profit (USD)"
  )

Interestingly, average profit is actually highest in January and December, and drops to its lowest point in the summer months of June and July. This seems counterintuitive given the season labels, but it makes sense when you think about it. In peak summer months, airlines run far more total flights, including many lower-demand routes that bring the overall average down. In quieter months, only the most reliable and profitable routes tend to operate, which pushes the average up. The red dashed line confirms that no month averages below zero, meaning flights are profitable across the whole year on average.

Conclusion

This analysis looked at what drives profitability across airline routes using 7,974 flights. A few clear patterns came out:

Demand level is the strongest predictor of profitability. High demand flights consistently outperform low demand ones in both profit and profit margin, more so than season alone.

Long-haul routes earn more money, but not because they are more efficient. When you look at profit margin instead of raw profit, the gap between route categories shrinks significantly.

Load factor matters a lot. Keeping seats filled is essential for covering fixed costs like fuel, crew, and airport fees. Flights with low load factors are far more likely to operate at a loss.

The monthly trend is counterintuitive. Average profit per flight is actually highest in winter months and lowest in summer, likely because peak season floods the schedule with lower-demand routes that drag the average down.

Overall, the data suggests that airlines should focus on maximizing demand and load factor rather than just adding long-haul routes. Efficiency matters as much as scale.
